title: Teaching LLMs Taste: How I Built an Automated Cycling Ride Recap with GoPro, Garmin, and Gemini
excerpt: Or as my wife says: How I managed to turn 3 hours of boring GoPro footage into 30 seconds of boring GoPro footage
content_html:
Or as my wife says: How I managed to turn 3 hours of boring GoPro footage into 30 seconds of boring GoPro footage

Road cycling has been my primary form of exercise, social outlet, therapy, wardrobe expense, and personality trait for almost a decade. I recently moved from Boston to New York, and my biggest anxiety was losing my 6:15am friend group that lapped to Dover or Blue Hills before work (WhatsApp group title: Boston WattsApp). Fortunately, the NYC cycling scene is awesome. I’ve made and rekindled many friendships, I have a new WattsApp group that’s great for Central Park laps before work, longer rides up 9W on the weekends, and the occasional Bear Mountain summit or 130+ mile ride to Montauk. There is no better way to start a day than 50 miles with friends and a cold brew + maple bacon log from Boxer Donut in Nyack.

Like many casual cyclists, I would do (or spend) anything to get faster at cycling, other than change my lifestyle in any way. I have all the tech and all the numbers: spandex, aero bike, carbon wheels, head unit, power meter, three separate heart rate monitors (Whoop, Garmin Watch, chest strap). Earlier this year I added a handlebar-mounted GoPro to the mix.

Of course, absolutely no one wants to watch 3 hours of being stuck behind Citibikes on West Side Highway. But I enjoy stitching together my favorite sections. I look through past footage, identify the fun parts, and put together a narrative to remember (remember, Strava is my personality). But since cycling is already time consuming, manually editing a highlight reel edit per ride was a nonstarter. So I built and open-sourced a pipeline called ride-recap to do it automatically.

Three months, 25 rides, 45 commits, and 1000 miles later, I can take hours of cycling footage plus a .fit file (time-series data with GPS, speed, heart rate, cadence, power, etc.) and turn it into a finished highlight reel. Every single second of the ride is scanned by gemini-3.5-flash, as is the clip ranking + curation. The whole thing costs about $0.04 per ride and takes 10 minutes. Here’s a finalized highlight:

Architecture

The system has six steps and one command: ride-recap process data/raw/<YYYY-MM-DD>/

  1. Ingest: pull GoPro data (.mp4 + .LRV) and Garmin .fit file into a ride folder
  2. Detect: four candidate sources identify interesting parts of the ride
    • Gemini vision: a two-pass frame scan for visually compelling segments (more on this later)
    • Telemetry: speed, climbs + descents, power + heart rate spikes
    • Strava API: popular segments on the route (e.g. State Line Sprint has 343 stars), this is the pavement every NYC cyclist knows
    • (Optional) Manual Labels: Streamlit app, occasionally I’ll hand-flag a moment as a “must include” (say, if I see a deer)
  3. Fuse: Generate candidate clips, rank them, penalize nearby moments (more on this later)
  4. (Optional) Review: Streamlit app, if I want to override the candidate ranking or rate clips
  5. Compose: Burn a ride telemetry overlay onto the selected clips
  6. Output: highlight_landscape.mp4 (60s, 16:9) for YouTube, highlight_portrait.mp4 (30s, 9:16) for Instagram

Here’s a pre/post for the step five burned overlay: you can see I’ve added live telemetry on the left, a GTA style mini-map + route trace on the right, and elevation profile + ride progress along the bottom.

What Makes Cycling Visuals Compelling?

Many have opined on the value of Taste™ in the post-AI era. Four months ago, I would have answered like Justice Potter Stewart describing pornography: I know it when I see it. I’ve now processed 180,430 seconds + frames through this pipeline. Here’s my strongest opinion on teaching taste to a LLM: taste develops through iteration count, you cannot specify taste in advance.

I started by sending every frame to Gemini with a prompt to “rate visual interest 1-5”. But a ride around New York could be interesting for so many reasons! A concrete jungle view from Hudson Yards or a Hudson River view from Peekskill, a climb up River Road or a descent down Bear Mountain, almost getting dropped in a paceline on 9W or almost getting hit by an e-bike going the wrong way on 9th Avenue.

Here’s an example of what I have in place now: every second of every ride goes through the same versioned prompt. I send Gemini six 848×480 proxy frames plus one line of telemetry, and I get one JSON object back. Flip through three calls from the July 12th ride below: a sprint through Hudson Yards, a beautiful view from under the GW Bridge, and two seconds stuck behind a black SUV. Any taste I developed came from me banging my head against JSON outputs I disagreed with.

The best way to improve a prompt is to fix its mistakes. That requires ground truth, so I created a Streamlit app and manually labeled a Central Park ride. I reviewed the entire ride frame-by-frame alongside telemetry graphs, and ranked around 30 moments I found interesting or boring, plus a description of why.

  • Gemini was impressed by any power output above 200W. I’m no Tadej Pogacar, but 200w isn’t that impressive.

    • Solution: athlete specific FTP/power zones injected into the system prompt. 200W means nothing without knowing who’s pushing the pedals.

      power_zones = (
    f"Athlete power zones (FTP={ftp}W):\n"
    f"  Z1 Active Recovery <{int(ftp * 0.55)}W | Z2 Endurance <{int(ftp * 0.75)}W | "
    f"Z3 Tempo <{int(ftp * 0.90)}W\n"
    f"  Z4 Threshold <{int(ftp * 1.05)}W | Z5 VO2max <{int(ftp * 1.20)}W | "
    f"Z6 Anaerobic <{int(ftp * 1.50)}W | Z7 Neuromuscular {int(ftp * 1.50)}W+\n"
    "Only Z5+ qualifies as genuinely hard effort. Z3-Z4 is moderate. Z1-Z2 is easy."
)
  • Gemini loved clips of tunnels, and being stuck behind cars

    • Solution: negative examples — a real intersection from the April 14th ride, immortalized in the prompt as a permanent warning

      Stopped behind a delivery truck at a red light, never moves:
{"light": 3, "composition": 2, "motion": 1, "scenery": 1, "subject": 1,
 "peak_offset": 0.5, "clip_type": "transition", "crop_x": 50,
 "reason": "Stuck behind delivery truck at red light"}
  • I liked identifying visuals: Brooklyn Bridge, State Line and 9W signs that are obvious to any cyclist

    • Solution: positive examples — show it what a 10 looks like, and what earns the score

      Cruising past Brooklyn Bridge at sunset, bridge biggest at end:
{"light": 9, "composition": 8, "motion": 5, "scenery": 10, "subject": 8,
 "peak_offset": 0.8, "clip_type": "landmark", "crop_x": 70,
 "reason": "Sunset Brooklyn Bridge approach, friend alongside"}
  • Gemini often picked two clips right next to one another
    • Solution: penalize nearby moments, enforce greedy selection across the ride (more below)
  • Gemini picked clips where I was going 0mph
    • Solution: filter clips below 5mph

Obvious in retrospect, but there’s no substitute other than looking at the clips Gemini selects, being highly opinionated about what should / should not be included, being specific enough about why, and repeating until you can’t think of anything more to improve. As Hamel writes: gathering supporting data and breaking down workflows into smaller units makes automated grading easier. You cannot teach an LLM taste if you do not have taste yourself.

Here are some outputs from an experiment I ran: I took a handful of stills from a real ride and scored them through every combination of prompts and models: from v2 to v10, the current 3.5-flash, two other Gemini models, and Opus 4.6. You can see that the prompt matters much more than more powerful foundation models.

I’ve iterated nine times on the ranking prompt so far. It is the most important file in the repo, and I treat it that way: the prompts are version controlled with a mandatory rationale explaining what failure mode the new version fixes. This prompt registry is the historical record of how an LLM’s taste for cycling visuals evolves.

This code is worth reading directly instead of describing. Check out how the prompt has grown: v2 is 20 lines and scores on two axes, v10 is 244 lines.

From Clips to a Reel

The candidate generation process ranks every single potential three second clip (around ~1000 per hour). The simplest approach to fill a 60 second reel is to select the top 20 ranked candidates. That results in an extremely repetitive edit, because the top ranked candidates are often right next to one another. A Bear Mountain descent is ~7 minutes, which results in ~100 different clips of a fast descent with beautiful views of the Hudson, all of which score highly on a per-clip basis, but are not independently interesting.

Teaching a LLM to accurately score one clip is a challenge, but at least each clip can be evaluated on its own. Candidate selection is even harder: How DOES a great editor select the footage to tell a great story? From my perspective, here are traits of a great NYC cycling edit:

  • Set the tone with the start and finish: for me, usually slow pedaling up West Side Highway, and back down through the skyscrapers of Hudson Yards
  • Order the clips chronologically, sampling uniformly across the ride (within reason)
  • Include landmarks, especially landmarks beloved by cyclists
  • Clips within a minute of one another are probably repetitive
  • Clips going <5mph are probably boring
  • Clips with friends in them are more interesting
  • Shoot for diversity: include the steepest climb, the fastest descent, the most powerful sprint, the sun shining through the forest in the Palisades, the NYC concrete jungle, The State Line sign, 9W Market

Similar to scoring, I’ve rebuilt the candidate fusion step six times, with each version fixing something from a prior iteration. The current selection process works like this:

  1. “Must include” manual labels are picked first
  2. A Gemini LLM narrative pass picks 20 clips to best tell the story of the ride, boosting “cross-source agreements” (if a human label + telemetry + Strava + Gemini all agree that a clip is interesting)
  3. Greedy re-ranking with a crowding penalty to avoid clips too close to something I’ve already picked
    1. First, a “quality” phase that picks best-first until the best remaining candidate is net-negative with crowding penalty
    2. Second, a “coverage” fill that guarantees the exact clip count by placing the liveliest available clips into the biggest timeline holes.

Here is the LLM narrative pass prompt and crowding penalty:

The 60 second July 12th highlight video at the top of this post is the output. Here’s a visual of the input: every candidate clip from the July 12th ride to 9W Market, plotted by time and score. The sprint you see as the closing shot is the orange dot on the right; the black SUV is the one at the bottom, ranked 70th of 70. Note that we didn’t pick the top 20 clips from the highest scoring section, but instead we selected 20 highly ranked clips spread somewhat evenly across the ride.

What’s Next?

I’m now three months of Claude Max, ~1000 miles, and $60 in Gemini API into this project. I have a YouTube channel with minimal traffic and a few pity subscribers, mostly related to me or in my WattsApp groups. I’ve gotten a few “wow, cool!” on my personal Instagram but again… mostly pity. I’m starting to feel done with this project, which scares me a bit! When I watch my videos, increasingly, they feel “right” to me, which might also mean my taste has plateaued and I’m complacent. I haven’t been iterating as quickly, and iteration is the whole key.

Obviously, I have registered a domain and have a harebrained business plan in my head. Hypothetically I could charge cyclists per ride to upload video footage + a .fit file and get back a finished edit.

Unfortunately, I think the TAM for customers who:

  • Road bike frequently
  • Are willing to buy a GoPro, Garmin, mount both to their bike, charge and record everything
  • Really want 60 second edits of their footage for Strava + Instagram
  • Do NOT want to make those edits themselves

Is one person, and it’s me.

But guess what! I love cycling, and I love building for me! Through a decade of managing data scientists, I have learned people are 10x more willing to go deep on something they are passionate about. This project scratched my itch. I’ve learned a ton about building LLM pipelines and coding agents. This post hasn’t even scratched the surface on what I’ve learned about:

  • Working with video files: .LRV proxy files, 5.3k HEVC, inter-frame compression, ffmpeg, and parallel processing to downscale and decode frames, VideoToolbox, RBGA overlays with Pillow.
  • Gemini billing, API failures, caching prompt results, identifying the cheapest model relative to a performance bar, evaluating foundation models within pipelines and as their own discrete steps, general token cost management.
  • Clock drift across GoPro + Garmin: the most brutally frustrating part of this project was when my offset math and the ride overlay felt off by a few seconds. I turned off wifi + bluetooth on the GoPro to save battery, and the clock drifts a few seconds per week, and occasionally within a ride.
  • Cropping a 16:9 frame (landscape) for a 9:16 (portrait) reel, and identifying the best place to crop the portrait (I only crop away from the middle to highlight other road cyclists in the frame).
  • Adding a blur-to-title opener + outro recap card.
  • “Out of distribution” clips: a deer, a dog doing Central Park laps, anything interesting for reasons that have nothing to do with cycling.
  • Calibrating LLM outputs (I used to have a light dimension that returned a 4 no matter what).

Some of my to-do list involves better evals for the learned ranker, testing non-Gemini models, learning more about video editing (color grading? motion blur? audio edits?). I’ve open-sourced ride-recap and will share it in a few communities, to see if anyone jumps on it or can push me in a new direction. New frontier models will come out, I’ll see what I can improve. There’s always more to do.

I now have personal software that I use a few times a week that brings me joy. The quality came through the iterations. This wouldn’t have been possible without coding agents, but it also wouldn’t have been possible without the version of “taste” I believe in now. Taste is not what you start with, it’s what you develop by watching thousands of three second clips, iterating, reviewing v25, being unsatisfied and shipping v26.

You are a cycling video editor reviewing GoPro handlebar-cam frames. Your job is to flag frames worth including in a highlight reel. VISUAL CUES for handlebar-cam footage: Strong: motion blur on pavement, corners with lean angle, rider wheels nearby, high-contrast light (shade/tunnels/reflections), landmarks (skylines/bridges/vistas), sprint bounce, urban elements rushing past, weather drama, close calls, unexpected events. Weak: steady cadence with nothing nearby, flat overcast light, stopped at lights. Score each frame on TWO dimensions: VISUAL (1-5): How does the frame look? Light, composition, landmarks. ACTION (1-5): How much is happening? Effort, proximity, speed, events. 5 = lead the edit. 4 = strong candidate. 3 = filler. 2 = unremarkable. 1 = dead. Assign ONE clip_type per frame: "scenery" | "action" | "urban" | "climb" | "descent" | "group_riding" | "incident" | "landmark" | "transition" Telemetry context is provided per batch — use it to disambiguate (plain frame at 350W = effort worth including). Omit frames where BOTH visual < {min_visual} AND action < {min_action}. Return ONLY a JSON array, no prose: [{{"frame_index": N, "visual": 1-5, "action": 1-5, "clip_type": "...", "reason": "max 10 words"}}] Return [] if nothing qualifies. I’ve open-sourced ride-recap and will share it in a few communities, to see if anyone jumps on it or can push me in a new direction. New frontier models will come out, I’ll see what I can improve. There’s always more to do. I now have personal software that I use a few times a week that brings me joy. The quality came through the iterations. This wouldn’t have been possible without coding agents, but it also wouldn’t have been possible without the version of “taste” I believe in now. Taste is not what you start with, it’s what you develop by watching thousands of three second clips, iterating, reviewing v25, being unsatisfied and shipping v26. 27 versions of the overlay, rendered on the same 3-second clip of the GW Bridge.

blocks.html_en: Road cycling has been my primary form of exercise, social outlet, therapy, wardrobe expense, and personality trait for almost a decade. I recently moved from Boston to New York, and my biggest anxiety was losing my 6:15am friend group that lapped to Dover or Blue Hills before work (WhatsApp group title: Boston WattsApp). Fortunately, the NYC cycling scene is awesome. I’ve made and rekindled many friendships, I have a new WattsApp group that’s great for Central Park laps before work, longer rides up 9W on the weekends, and the occasional Bear Mountain summit or 130+ mile ride to Montauk. There is no better way to start a day than 50 miles with friends and a cold brew + maple bacon log from Boxer Donut in Nyack.

blocks.html_en: Like many casual cyclists, I would do (or spend) anything to get faster at cycling, other than change my lifestyle in any way. I have all the tech and all the numbers: spandex, aero bike, carbon wheels, head unit, power meter, three separate heart rate monitors (Whoop, Garmin Watch, chest strap). Earlier this year I added a handlebar-mounted GoPro to the mix.

blocks.html_en: Of course, absolutely no one wants to watch 3 hours of being stuck behind Citibikes on West Side Highway. But I enjoy stitching together my favorite sections. I look through past footage, identify the fun parts, and put together a narrative to remember (remember, Strava is my personality). But since cycling is already time consuming, manually editing a highlight reel edit per ride was a nonstarter. So I built and open-sourced a pipeline called ride-recap to do it automatically.

blocks.html_en: <figcaption data-astro-cid-bj3fsypb="">Candidate generation from four different sources (GoPro video, Garmin telemetry, Strava segments, manual labels) fused into one chronological story.</figcaption>

sentences.en: Candidate generation from four different sources (GoPro video, Garmin telemetry, Strava segments, manual labels) fused into one chronological story.

blocks.html_en: • Ingest: pull GoPro data (.mp4 + .LRV) and Garmin .fit file into a ride folder

sentences.en: Ingest: pull GoPro data (.mp4 + .LRV) and Garmin .fit file into a ride folder
sentences.zh: 摄取：将 GoPro 数据 (.mp4 + .LRV) 和 Garmin .fit 文件提取到骑行文件夹中

blocks.html_en:
• Detect: four candidate sources identify interesting parts of the ride
    • Gemini vision: a two-pass frame scan for visually compelling segments (more on this later)
    • Telemetry: speed, climbs + descents, power + heart rate spikes
    • Strava API: popular segments on the route (e.g. State Line Sprint has 343 stars), this is the pavement every NYC cyclist knows
    • (Optional) Manual Labels: Streamlit app, occasionally I’ll hand-flag a moment as a “must include” (say, if I see a deer)

sentences.en: Detect: four candidate sources identify interesting parts of the ride Gemini vision: a two-pass frame scan for visually compelling segments (more on this later) Telemetry: speed, climbs + descents, power + heart rate spikes Strava API: popular segments on the route (e.g. State Line Sprint has 343 stars), this is the pavement every NYC cyclist knows (Optional) Manual Labels: Streamlit app, occasionally I’ll hand-flag a moment as a “must include” (say, if I see a deer)
sentences.zh: 检测：四个候选源识别骑行中有趣的部分 Gemini 视觉：两次通过帧扫描，寻找视觉上引人注目的路段（稍后详细介绍） 遥测：速度、爬坡 + 下坡、功率 + 心率峰值 Strava API：路线上的热门路段（例如 State Line Sprint 有 343 颗星），这是每个纽约市骑车人都知道的人行道（可选） 手动标签：Streamlit 应用程序，偶尔我会手动标记某个时刻为“必须”包括”（例如，如果我看到一只鹿）

blocks.html_en: Many have opined on the value of Taste™ in the post-AI era. Four months ago, I would have answered like Justice Potter Stewart describing pornography: I know it when I see it. I’ve now processed 180,430 seconds + frames through this pipeline. Here’s my strongest opinion on teaching taste to a LLM: taste develops through iteration count, you cannot specify taste in advance.

sentences.en: Many have opined on the value of Taste™ in the post-AI era.
sentences.zh: 许多人都对 Taste™ 在后人工智能时代的价值发表了看法。

sentences.zh: 四个月前，我会像波特·斯图尔特法官描述色情作品一样回答：我一看到它就知道它。

sentences.en: I’ve now processed 180,430 seconds + frames through this pipeline.
sentences.zh: 我现在已经通过这个管道处理了 180,430 秒+帧。

sentences.en: Here’s my strongest opinion on teaching taste to a LLM: taste develops through iteration count, you cannot specify taste in advance.
sentences.zh: 以下是我对向法学硕士教授品味的最强烈观点：品味是通过迭代计数发展的，你无法提前指定品味。

blocks.html_en: I started by sending every frame to Gemini with a prompt to “rate visual interest 1-5”. But a ride around New York could be interesting for so many reasons! A concrete jungle view from Hudson Yards or a Hudson River view from Peekskill, a climb up River Road or a descent down Bear Mountain, almost getting dropped in a paceline on 9W or almost getting hit by an e-bike going the wrong way on 9th Avenue.

sentences.en: I started by sending every frame to Gemini with a prompt to “rate visual interest 1-5”.
sentences.zh: 我首先将每一帧发送给 Gemini，并提示“对视觉兴趣进行评分 1-5”。

sentences.en: But a ride around New York could be interesting for so many reasons!
sentences.zh: 但出于多种原因，在纽约兜风可能会很有趣！

sentences.en: A concrete jungle view from Hudson Yards or a Hudson River view from Peekskill, a climb up River Road or a descent down Bear Mountain, almost getting dropped in a paceline on 9W or almost getting hit by an e-bike going the wrong way on 9th Avenue.
sentences.zh: 从哈德逊庭院 (Hudson Yards) 欣赏混凝土丛林景观，或从皮克斯基尔 (Peekskill) 欣赏哈德逊河景观，爬上河路 (River Road) 或下熊山 (Bear Mountain)，差点在 9W 的配速线上掉下去，或者差点被第 9 大道上逆行的电动自行车撞到。

blocks.html_en: Here’s an example of what I have in place now: every second of every ride goes through the same versioned prompt. I send Gemini six 848×480 proxy frames plus one line of telemetry, and I get one JSON object back. Flip through three calls from the July 12th ride below: a sprint through Hudson Yards, a beautiful view from under the GW Bridge, and two seconds stuck behind a black SUV. Any taste I developed came from me banging my head against JSON outputs I disagreed with.

sentences.zh: 这是我现在所采取的示例：每次骑行的每一秒都会经历相同版本的提示。

sentences.en: I send Gemini six 848×480 proxy frames plus one line of telemetry, and I get one JSON object back.

sentences.en: Any taste I developed came from me banging my head against JSON outputs I disagreed with.
sentences.zh: 我所形成的任何品味都来自于我对不同意的 JSON 输出的反复思考。

sentences.en: This Gemini call caught a sprint through Hudson Yards: rated 8s on motion, scenery, and subject, and shipped as the closing shot.
sentences.zh: 这个双子座的电话在哈德逊广场进行了一次冲刺：在动作、风景和主题上评分为 8 分，并作为最后一个镜头发布。

blocks.html_en: The best way to improve a prompt is to fix its mistakes. That requires ground truth, so I created a Streamlit app and manually labeled a Central Park ride. I reviewed the entire ride frame-by-frame alongside telemetry graphs, and ranked around 30 moments I found interesting or boring, plus a description of why.

sentences.en: The best way to improve a prompt is to fix its mistakes.
sentences.zh: 改进提示的最好方法是纠正它的错误。

sentences.zh: 这需要基本事实，因此我创建了一个 Streamlit 应用程序并手动标记中央公园骑行。

sentences.zh: 我根据遥测图逐帧回顾了整个骑行过程，并对我认为有趣或无聊的大约 30 个时刻进行了排名，并说明了原因。

sentences.en: The Streamlit labeler: 18 clips, 207 minutes, 3134 frames.
sentences.zh: Streamlit 标签机：18 个剪辑，207 分钟，3134 帧。

sentences.en: Video up top, ride telemetry along the bottom, I can compare my labels against Gemini scores and see where they diverge.
sentences.zh: 视频位于顶部，遥测位于底部，我可以将我的标签与 Gemini 分数进行比较，看看它们的差异在哪里。

blocks.html_en: Obvious in retrospect, but there’s no substitute other than looking at the clips Gemini selects, being highly opinionated about what should / should not be included, being specific enough about why, and repeating until you can’t think of anything more to improve. blocks.html_en: The candidate generation process ranks every single potential three second clip (around ~1000 per hour). The simplest approach to fill a 60 second reel is to select the top 20 ranked candidates. That results in an extremely repetitive edit, because the top ranked candidates are often right next to one another. A Bear Mountain descent is ~7 minutes, which results in ~100 different clips of a fast descent with beautiful views of the Hudson, all of which score highly on a per-clip basis, but are not independently interesting.

    2. Second, a “coverage” fill that guarantees the exact clip count by placing the liveliest available clips into the biggest timeline holes.

blocks.html_en: I’m now three months of Claude Max, ~1000 miles, and $60 in Gemini API into this project. I have a YouTube channel with minimal traffic and a few pity subscribers, mostly related to me or in my WattsApp groups. I’ve gotten a few “wow, cool!” on my personal Instagram but again… mostly pity. I’m starting to feel done with this project, which scares me a bit! When I watch my videos, increasingly, they feel “right” to me, which might also mean my taste has plateaued and I’m complacent. I haven’t been iterating as quickly, and iteration is the whole key.

content_html_zh:
或者正如我妻子所说：我如何将 3 小时无聊的 GoPro 镜头变成 30 秒无聊的 GoPro 镜头

近十年来，公路自行车一直是我锻炼、社交、治疗、衣柜开支和个性特征的主要形式。 我最近从波士顿搬到了纽约，我最大的焦虑是失去早上 6:15 上班前去多佛或蓝山的朋友群（WhatsApp 群名称：Boston WattsApp）。 幸运的是，纽约市的自行车运动非常棒。 我结交并重新点燃了许多友谊，我有一个新的 WattsApp 小组，非常适合上班前在中央公园绕圈、周末进行 9W 的长途骑行，以及偶尔攀登熊山或前往蒙托克 130 多英里的骑行。 没有什么比和朋友一起行驶 50 英里，喝一杯来自尼亚克 Boxer Donut 的冷萃啤酒 + 枫木培根原木更好的方式开始新的一天了。

像许多休闲自行车手一样，除了以任何方式改变我的生活方式之外，我会做（或花）任何事情来提高骑自行车速度。 我拥有所有的技术和所有的数字：氨纶、气动自行车、碳轮、主机、功率计、三个独立的心率监测器（Whoop、Garmin 手表、胸带）。 今年早些时候，我添加了一个安装在车把上的 GoPro。

当然，绝对没有人愿意看到在西区高速公路上被困在 Citibikes 后面 3 个小时。 但我喜欢将我最喜欢的部分拼接在一起。 我浏览过去的镜头，找出有趣的部分，并整理出一个故事来记住（记住，Strava 是我的个性）。 但由于骑自行车已经很耗时，因此每次骑行手动编辑精彩片段是不可能的。 因此，我构建并开源了一个名为ride-recap 的管道来自动执行此操作。

三个月、25 次骑行、45 次承诺和 1000 英里后，我可以拍摄几个小时的骑行镜头和 .fit 文件（带有 GPS、速度、心率、节奏、功率等的时间序列数据），并将其转换为成品精彩片段。 骑行的每一秒都会由gemini-3.5-flash 扫描，剪辑排名+管理也是如此。 每次乘车费用约为 0.04 美元，全程需要 10 分钟。 这是最终的亮点：

建筑学

该系统有六个步骤和一个命令：ride-recap process data/raw/<YYYY-MM-DD>/

  1. 摄取：将 GoPro 数据 (.mp4 + .LRV) 和 Garmin .fit 文件提取到骑行文件夹中
  2. 检测：四个候选源识别骑行中有趣的部分 Gemini 视觉：两次通过帧扫描，寻找视觉上引人注目的路段（稍后详细介绍） 遥测：速度、爬坡 + 下坡、功率 + 心率峰值 Strava API：路线上的热门路段（例如 State Line Sprint 有 343 颗星），这是每个纽约市骑车人都知道的人行道（可选） 手动标签：Streamlit 应用程序，偶尔我会手动标记某个时刻为“必须”包括”（例如，如果我看到一只鹿）
  3. Fuse：生成候选剪辑，对它们进行排名，惩罚附近的时刻（稍后会详细介绍）
  4. （可选）评论：Streamlit 应用程序，如果我想覆盖候选排名或评分剪辑
  5. 撰写：将骑行遥测覆盖刻录到选定的剪辑上
  6. 输出：highlight_landscape.mp4（60s，16:9）用于 YouTube，highlight_portrait.mp4（30s，9:16）用于 Instagram

这是第五步烧录覆盖的前/后：您可以看到我在左侧添加了实时遥测，在右侧添加了 GTA 风格的迷你地图 + 路线跟踪，以及在底部添加了海拔剖面 + 骑行进度。

是什么让骑行视觉如此引人注目？

许多人都对 Taste™ 在后人工智能时代的价值发表了看法。 四个月前，我会像波特·斯图尔特法官描述色情作品一样回答：我一看到它就知道它。 我现在已经通过这个管道处理了 180,430 秒+帧。 以下是我对向法学硕士教授品味的最强烈观点：品味是通过迭代计数发展的，你无法提前指定品味。

我首先将每一帧发送给 Gemini，并提示“对视觉兴趣进行评分 1-5”。 但出于多种原因，在纽约兜风可能会很有趣！ 从哈德逊庭院 (Hudson Yards) 欣赏混凝土丛林景观，或从皮克斯基尔 (Peekskill) 欣赏哈德逊河景观，爬上河路 (River Road) 或下熊山 (Bear Mountain)，差点在 9W 的配速线上掉下去，或者差点被第 9 大道上逆行的电动自行车撞到。

这是我现在所采取的示例：每次骑行的每一秒都会经历相同版本的提示。 我向 Gemini 发送了 6 个 848×480 代理帧以及一行遥测数据，然后我得到了一个 JSON 对象。 浏览下面 7 月 12 日骑行的三个电话：冲刺穿过哈德逊广场、从 GW 大桥下欣赏美丽的景色，以及在一辆黑色 SUV 后面停留两秒。 我所形成的任何品味都来自于我对不同意的 JSON 输出的反复思考。

改进提示的最好方法是纠正它的错误。 这需要基本事实，因此我创建了一个 Streamlit 应用程序并手动标记中央公园骑行。 我根据遥测图逐帧回顾了整个骑行过程，并对我认为有趣或无聊的大约 30 个时刻进行了排名，并说明了原因。

  • Gemini 对任何超过 200W 的功率输出都印象深刻。 我不是 Tadej Pogacar，但 200w 并没有那么令人印象深刻。 解决方案：将运动员特定的 FTP/功率区域注入系统提示中。 如果不知道谁在踩踏板，200W 就毫无意义。 power_zones = ( f"运动员功率区 (FTP={ftp}W):\n" f" Z1 主动恢复 <{int(ftp * 0.55)}W | Z2 耐力 <{int(ftp * 0.75)}W | " f"Z3 节奏 <{int(ftp * 0.90)}W\n" f" Z4 阈值 <{int(ftp * 1.05)}W | Z5 VO2max <{int(ftp * 1.20)}W | " f"Z6 无氧 <{int(ftp * 1.50)}W | Z7 神经肌肉 {int(ftp * 1.50)}W+\n" “只有 Z5+ 才符合真正的努力程度。 Z3-Z4适中。 Z1-Z2 很简单。”）
  • 双子座喜欢隧道片段，以及被困在汽车后面 解决方案：反面例子 - 4 月 14 日骑行中的真实十字路口，作为永久警告在提示中永垂不朽 红灯时停在送货卡车后面，永远不会移动：{"light": 3, "composition": 2, "motion": 1, "scenery": 1, "subject": 1, "peak_offset": 0.5, "clip_type": "transition", "crop_x": 50, "reason": "红灯时被困在送货卡车后面"}
  • 我喜欢识别视觉效果：布鲁克林大桥、State Line 和 9W 标志，这些标志对任何骑自行车的人来说都是显而易见的。 解决方案：正面例子 - 展示 10 是什么样子，以及如何获得分数 日落时巡航经过布鲁克林大桥，桥最大的末端：{"light": 9, "composition": 8, "motion": 5, "scenery": 10, "subject": 8, "peak_offset": 0.8, "clip_type": "landmark", "crop_x": 70, "reason": "日落布鲁克林大桥，朋友在旁边"}
  • 双子座经常选择两个相邻的剪辑解决方案：惩罚附近的时刻，在整个骑行过程中强制执行贪婪选择（更多内容见下文）
  • Gemini 选择了我要去 0 英里/小时的剪辑 解决方案：过滤低于 5 英里/小时的剪辑

回想起来很明显，但除了查看双子座选择的剪辑，对应该/不应该包含哪些内容有很强的主见，足够具体地说明原因，然后重复，直到你想不出还有什么可以改进的为止，没有其他替代方案。 正如哈梅尔所写：收集支持数据并将工作流程分解为更小的单元可以使自动评分变得更容易。 如果你自己没有品味，你就无法教授法学硕士学位。

以下是我进行的实验的一些输出：我从真实的骑行中拍摄了一些静态图像，并通过提示和模型的每种组合对它们进行评分：从 v2 到 v10、当前的 3.5 闪光灯、其他两个 Gemini 模型和 Opus 4.6。 您可以看到提示比更强大的基础模型更重要。

到目前为止，我已经对排名提示进行了九次迭代。 它是存储库中最重要的文件，我是这样对待它的：提示是版本控制的，并带有强制说明新版本修复的故障模式的基本原理。 这个提示注册表是法学硕士对自行车视觉效果的品味如何演变的历史记录。

这段代码值得直接阅读而不是描述。 看看提示是如何增长的：v2 是 20 行，在两个轴上有分数，v10 是 244 行。

从剪辑到卷轴

候选生成过程对每个潜在的三秒剪辑进行排名（每小时约 1000 个）。 填充 60 秒卷轴的最简单方法是选择排名前 20 名的候选者。 这会导致极其重复的编辑，因为排名靠前的候选人往往彼此相邻。 熊山下降大约需要 7 分钟，这会产生大约 100 个不同的快速下降片段，并欣赏哈德逊河的美丽景色，所有这些片段在每个片段的基础上得分都很高，但独立起来并不有趣。

教法学硕士对一个片段进行准确评分是一项挑战，但至少每个片段都可以单独评估。 候选者的选择更加困难：优秀的剪辑师如何选择素材来讲述一个精彩的故事？ 从我的角度来看，以下是出色的纽约自行车编辑的特点：

  • 为开始和结束定下基调：对我来说，通常是缓慢地沿着西区高速公路骑行，然后穿过哈德逊城市广场的摩天大楼返回
  • 按时间顺序排列剪辑，在整个骑行过程中均匀采样（在合理范围内）
  • 包括地标，尤其是骑自行车者喜爱的地标
  • 彼此相隔一分钟内的剪辑可能是重复的
  • 行驶速度低于 5 英里/小时的剪辑可能很无聊
  • 有朋友在里面的剪辑更有趣
  • 追求多样性：包括最陡的攀登、最快的下降、最有力的冲刺、穿过 Palisades 森林的阳光、纽约市的混凝土丛林、State Line 标志、9W 市场

与评分类似，我已经重建了候选融合步骤六次，每个版本都修复了先前迭代中的某些内容。 当前的选择过程是这样的：

  1. 首先选择“必须包括”手动标签
  2. Gemini LLM 叙事通行证会挑选 20 个剪辑来最好地讲述骑行故事，从而促进“跨源协议”（如果人类标签 + 遥测 + Strava + Gemini 都同意某个剪辑很有趣）
  3. 贪婪的重新排名与拥挤惩罚，以避免剪辑太接近我已经选择的东西首先，“质量”阶段，首先选择最好的，直到最好的剩余候选者因拥挤惩罚而净负。其次，“覆盖”填充，通过将最活跃的可用剪辑放入最大的时间线孔中来保证准确的剪辑计数。

以下是LLM叙事通过提示和拥挤惩罚：

本文顶部的第 60 个 7 月 12 日精彩视频就是输出。 以下是输入的视觉效果：从 7 月 12 日骑行到 9W Market 的每个候选者剪辑，按时间和分数绘制。 您在最后镜头中看到的冲刺是右侧的橙色点；黑色 SUV 垫底，在 70 辆汽车中排名第 70 位。 请注意，我们并没有从得分最高的部分中挑选前 20 个片段，而是选择了 20 个排名较高的片段，这些片段在骑行过程中均匀分布。

接下来是什么？

我现在已经用三个月的 Claude Max、大约 1000 英里和 60 美元的 Gemini API 投入到这个项目中。 我有一个 YouTube 频道，流量很少，订阅者也很少，大部分与我或我的 WattsApp 群组相关。 我得到了一些“哇，太酷了！” 在我的个人 Instagram 上，但再次…… 主要是可惜。 我开始觉得这个项目已经完成了，这让我有点害怕！ 当我观看我的视频时，越来越多地觉得它们对我来说“正确”，这也可能意味着我的品味已经趋于稳定并且我感到自满。 我的迭代速度没有那么快，而迭代是关键。

显然，我已经注册了一个域名，并且脑子里有一个轻率的商业计划。 假设我可以向骑行者收取每次骑行的费用，以上传视频片段 + .fit 文件并返回完成的编辑。

不幸的是，我认为 TAM 适合以下客户：

  • 经常骑公路自行车
  • 愿意购买 GoPro、Garmin，将它们安装到自行车上、充电并记录一切
  • 真的想要在 Strava + Instagram 上对他们的镜头进行 60 秒的编辑
  • 不想自己进行这些编辑

是一个人，而且是我。

但你猜怎么着！ 我喜欢骑自行车，也喜欢为我自己建造！ 通过十年管理数据科学家的经历，我了解到人们更愿意深入研究他们所热衷的事物，这种意愿要高出十倍。 这个项目让我心痒痒。 我学到了很多关于构建 LLM 管道和编码代理的知识。 这篇文章甚至没有触及我所了解的表面：

  • 处理视频文件：.LRV 代理文件、5.3k HEVC、帧间压缩、ffmpeg 以及缩小和解码帧的并行处理、VideoToolbox、带 Pillow 的 RBGA 覆盖。
  • Gemini 计费、API 故障、缓存提示结果、识别相对于性能条的最便宜模型、评估管道内的基础模型及其自己的离散步骤、一般令牌成本管理。
  • GoPro + Garmin 之间的时钟漂移：这个项目中最令人沮丧的部分是当我的偏移数学和骑行叠加感觉有几秒钟的偏差时。 我关闭了 GoPro 上的 WiFi + 蓝牙以节省电池，时钟每周都会漂移几秒钟，偶尔会在骑行过程中漂移。
  • 将 16:9 帧（横向）裁剪为 9:16（肖像）卷轴，并确定裁剪肖像的最佳位置（我仅从中间裁剪以突出显示帧中的其他公路自行车手）。
  • 添加模糊标题开场白 + 结尾回顾卡。
  • “分发完毕”的片段：一只鹿、一只狗在中央公园绕圈，以及任何与自行车无关的有趣的事情。
  • 校准 LLM 输出（我曾经有一个无论如何都返回 4 的光维度）。

我的一些待办事项列表涉及对学习排名器进行更好的评估、测试非 Gemini 模型、了解有关视频编辑的更多信息（颜色分级？ 运动模糊？ 音频编辑？）。 我已经开源了骑行回顾，并将在几个社区中分享它，看看是否有人参与其中或可以将我推向新的方向。 新的前沿模型将会出现，我会看看我可以改进什么。 总是有更多事情要做。

我现在拥有每周使用几次的个人软件，它给我带来了快乐。 质量是通过迭代而来的。 如果没有编码代理，这是不可能的，但如果没有我现在相信的“品味”版本，这也是不可能的。 品味不是你一开始的，而是你通过观看数千个三秒剪辑、迭代、审查 v25、不满意并发布 v26 来开发的。
